title: Charcuterie Serving Board
summary: A scrap-wood grazing board built from angled offcuts framed in 1x2 — a genuinely good use for the odd short pieces left over from other projects.
description:
Every piece in the field of the board is cut at the same 30°-off-square angle, with both ends parallel — the long-point-to-short-point measurement is what actually becomes the board's finished depth, so that's the number to set, not the overall board length. A stop block on the miter saw is worth setting up even for a handful of cuts, since any piece that comes out a hair short leaves a visible gap once everything is glued and framed.

This is one of the best scrap-bin projects in the catalog: odds and ends of 1x and 2x stock, cut to the same depth and glued up with varying widths, create the layered channels and sections that give a grazing board its texture — no single board wide enough for the whole thing is required or even desirable.

tools.note: Set to a 30°-off-square angle for every field piece.
Trims the assembled board's ends flush.

materials.name: Scrap 1x and 2x lumber
materials.note: Odds and ends left over from other projects; free if already on hand.

materials.name: Pine, 1x2, scraps
materials.note: Frames the outside edge of the board.

materials.name: Food-safe polyurethane
materials.note: Protects the board and makes it safe to wipe clean after serving food.

cutList.note: Both ends cut parallel at 30° off square; long point to short point equals the board's finished depth (16" shown as an example).
Stood upright against the saw fence to match the field pieces' angle.
Cut a little long, then trimmed flush after assembly.

steps.title: Cut the field pieces
steps.body:
Set your miter saw to a 30° off-square angle. Cut scrap wood pieces with both ends parallel at this angle — the long-point-to-short-point measurement equals your board's finished depth. For example, for a 16"-deep board, cut the pieces 16" long, long point to short point, both ends parallel at 30° off square.

It's important that every piece is the same length — use a stop block on the saw so all the pieces come out identical.

steps.title: Lay out the pattern
steps.body: As you cut, lay the field pieces out to create varied depths and channels across the board. Cut the divider strips standing upright against the saw fence, so they pick up the same angle as the field pieces.

steps.title: Attach the first end frame
steps.body: Cut an end frame piece a little longer than needed. Attach it to the ends of the field pieces with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue, applying glue between the field pieces as well. Keep all edges flush to the bottom.

steps.title: Attach the opposite end frame
steps.body: Repeat the previous step on the opposite side.

steps.title: Trim the ends flush
steps.body: Mark the ends to be trimmed with a square, checking there are no nails in the cut path. Trim the ends flush with a circular saw, or a miter saw if the board is small enough to fit.

steps.title: Cap the trimmed ends
steps.body: Cut short 1x2 end caps and nail and glue them to the freshly trimmed ends.

steps.title: Finish the board
steps.body: Finish as desired. If the board will be used for serving food, apply a good coat of food-safe polyurethane so it can be wiped clean after use and the finish protects the food from the stain underneath.

images.alt: Scrap wood charcuterie serving board with angled channels
